content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PRACTrcss C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 c 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
December 6,2001
Marilyn Farley
827 CoventryLane
Fairfield, California 94533
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-275
Dear Ms. Farley:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Because your request is for general advice
and does not relate to either an existing governmental office or a specific governmental decision,
we treat it as a request for general and informal assistance only.2 Our assistance is limited to
provisions of the Act and does not address areas of the law outside the Act, such as the doctrine
of incompatible offices.
QUESTTONS
If you are appointed to the city council for the City of Fairfield
1. What conflicts might arise because of your husband's position as a sitting county
supervisor for the County of Solano?
2. What general conflict-of-interest issues might arise for you?
I Government Code sections 8i000-91014. All statutoryreferences are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections 18009-18997. All references
to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83 I 14, Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)
g


File No. I-01-275
Page2
CONCLUSION
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from a
public official's participation in governmental decisions affecting his or her economic interests.
Absent specific information, we cannot advise you regarding potential conflicts of interest in any
particular governmental decision. Your husband's representation of the County of Solano as a
supervisor does not in and of itself create a conflict of interest.
FACTS
Due to the recent election of a sitting council member to mayor, a seat has become vacant
on the Fairfield City Council that the city plans to fill by appointment. You have applied for the
vacancy. Because your husband is a county supervisor representing Solano County's Third
Supervisorial District, which includes large portions of the City of Fairfield, you are concerned
about possible conflicts of interest that may arise.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests (Section
81001(b)) and, specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public officiat from making, particrpating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted a standard analysis for
deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest (Regulation 18700, subdivisions
(b)(1) - (8), which is discussed in the enclosed pamphlet entitled "Can I Vote?" The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that may affect one or more of his or her financial interests.
A public official's income, for purposes of the Act, includes his or her community property
interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Generally, a public official's
community property interest in his or her spouse's income would be considered income to the
public official, and the public official would have an economic interest in the source of that
income. (Ibid; Section 87103(c).) However, salary and reimbursement for expenses from a state,
local, or federal government agency are expressly excluded from the Act's definition of "income."
(Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, both your and your husband's salary and reimbursement for
expenses received from local government agencies are not considered income for purposes of the
Act. (Section 87103(c); Section 82030(b)(2).) Therefore, as a general rule, your husband's
representation of the County of Solano as a supervisor would not in and of itself create a conflict
of interest for you or for him. Despite this exception, however, a public official may not make,
participate in making or use his or her official position to influence decisions to hire, fire,
promote, demote, or discipline his or her spouse, or set the spouse's salary to a level that is
different from salaries paid to other employees, in the same job classification or position.


File No. I-01-275
Page 3
(Regulation 18705.5(b).) However, you have not stated in your letter whether your husband as a
county supervisor could be involved in appointment and personnel decisions regarding the
Fairfield City Council. Thus, we don't further analyze this potential conflict.
Because it is impossible for us to predict other financial interests which you may have that
might be affected by either you or your husband in your respective governmental positions, we
cannot proceed with the analysis. As specific issues arise and you have further questions, please
do not hesitate to ask us for advice. If you have any questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By
Adrianne Korchmaros
P olitical Reform Consultant
Enclosure
